lesson.menu_desc: Tipping, hidden costs & the advanced passive -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 16 -- Money Matters Abroad
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Pricila Adamo | Aula 16 -- Money Matters Abroad

listenings.text: Good afternoon, and welcome aboard. Before we begin today's tour, a quick word about money. Your ticket has already been paid, so nothing more is owed for the ride itself. However, please be aware that a small service charge is added at the two restaurants we visit, and tips are not included in that charge. At the craft market, prices are usually marked up for visitors, so a little bargaining is expected and never taken badly. If you pay by card, you may be charged a foreign transaction fee by your own bank, not by us. To keep things simple, a flat rate of twenty euros covers all entrance tickets for the day. Loose change is always useful for the small things. Enjoy the tour, and please ask if anything is unclear.

The hardest lesson I learned about money abroad was not how to count it, but how it is handled differently everywhere. On my first trip, I assumed tipping worked the same as at home. It does not. In some countries, a service charge is included and nothing extra is expected. In others, the staff are barely paid, and a tip is quietly relied on. I once left nothing, because I thought the bill had been settled in full, and I was given a very cold look. Now I always ask one simple question before I pay: is service included? If the answer is yes, I relax. If it is no, I add a little gratuity. A few coins, asked about politely, are never wasted. Money is spoken about so rarely that one honest question can save you a great deal of embarrassment.

reading.rtitle: The Quiet Rules of Money Abroad
paras: Few things are governed by such unwritten rules as money. A traveler who has mastered the language of a country can still be caught out by the way its bills are handled. In one city the service is added automatically and a tip would be refused; in the next, the same gesture is quietly expected and its absence is noticed at once. The price on the menu is rarely the price that is paid, and the difference -- a service charge here, a surcharge there -- is seldom explained until the bill arrives.

Tourists, it is said, are overcharged more often than they realize. A markup is added the moment an accent is heard, and a hidden cost is buried somewhere in the small print of the receipt. Cards are not always welcome, and when they are accepted, a foreign fee may be charged by the bank rather than the shop. The wise traveler is told, again and again, to carry a little petty cash and some loose change, and to ask one blunt question before paying: is service included, or is a gratuity expected on top?

None of this is meant to alarm. It is simply how the world works once you cross a border, and it is handled best with curiosity rather than fear. Budget a daily allowance, agree a flat rate where you can, and never be afraid to ask why a number is what it is. Money, after all, is only spoken about openly by those who are not embarrassed by it -- and those, it turns out, are the travelers who are rarely caught out.
reading.source: Adapted from a travel-economics feature, The Wanderer Review

gist.prompt: What is the writer's main point about money while traveling?
choices: Travelers should never use cards abroad and only ever pay in cash.
Money is governed by unwritten local rules -- tipping, charges and markups differ everywhere -- so the wise traveler stays curious and simply asks.
Tourists are always cheated, so the only safe option is to refuse to pay any extra charges.

items: The price printed on the menu is usually the exact price that is paid.
f
The price on the menu is rarely the price that is paid; charges are added later.
According to the writer, a markup is sometimes added as soon as a tourist's accent is heard.
t
A markup is added the moment an accent is heard.
A foreign card fee is always charged by the shop, never by the bank.
f
A foreign fee may be charged by the bank rather than the shop.
The writer says the best attitude toward these money rules is curiosity, not fear.
t
It is handled best with curiosity rather than fear.

modals.title: How likely is it? (talking about money rules)
cards: A fixed rule with no doubt. The service charge is built into the bill, so nothing extra is needed.
A service charge is added automatically.
It happens every time, by default. You do not choose it; the restaurant adds it for you.
A fee may be charged abroad.
It might happen, it might not -- it depends on your bank. Use may or might when you are not sure.
Not certain, but worth expecting. Many cards are blocked the first time they are used in a new country.
You won't be charged for the ticket again.
It cannot happen because it is already paid. Use won't be for something that will NOT occur.

matching.title: Match each money expression to its meaning
defs: An extra fee buried in the small print that you do not see until later
A percentage added to the bill to pay the staff for their service
A small amount of cash kept for minor, everyday spending
The amount a seller adds on top of the cost, often more for tourists
A fixed sum of money you allow yourself to spend each day
An additional charge added on top of the normal price
One single fixed price that covers everything, not per item
A tip given to thank someone for good service

vocabnote.text: Agentless passive: when the doer is obvious or unimportant, we drop it. "A service charge is added" -- we don't care who adds it. Use by + agent only when the doer is new information: "You may be charged a fee by your bank" -- here the bank matters, so we keep it.

rephrase.title: Rewrite each active sentence in the passive
items: They add a service charge to every bill.
agentless
They overcharged us at the market.
agentless, past
The bank will charge you a foreign fee.
by + agent
They have included the tip in the total.
agentless, present perfect
People say that tourists pay more.
It is said that...

answerkey.title: Reveal the passive answer key
list: A service charge is added to every bill.
We were overcharged at the market.
You will be charged a foreign fee by the bank.
The tip has been included in the total.
It is said that tourists pay more.
answerkey.note: The passive is be + past participle. Drop the doer (agentless) when it is obvious or unimportant; keep by + agent only when the doer is new or important. Match the tense of the original: were (past), will be (future), has been (present perfect).

analyse.title: Think it through
items: Why do advice texts about money use the passive so often (service is added, tourists are overcharged) instead of naming who does it?
How might the tipping rules the writer describes differ between a country where service is always included and one where staff are barely paid?
The writer says honest travelers who ask openly are rarely caught out. Why might simply asking Is service included? protect you from hidden costs?
